persons.arguments_against: Strongest case against: Archibald is a common surname here (16 officials, 12 biographies), the deterministic position check FAILed, and the 1889 staff-list row shows 'M.P.', an office the biography never mentions. But the counter-hypothesis requires a second 'Adams G. Archibald' — 'Adams' is a highly distinctive forename, printed in full in the stint key — who also held a K.C.M.G. first appearing in the lists in 1886 (the bio's award is dated 1885: 'K.C.M.G. (1885)') AND was associated with the Province of Nova Scotia, where the bio's subject was Lieutenant-Governor 1873-83. The M.P. row does not contradict: an ex-Lieutenant-Governor entering the Commons after 1883 is consistent, and the row still carries his K.C.M.G. The position FAIL is mechanical (most staff-list rows print no position at all — they are Privy Council honours-roll entries, expected for an honoured ex-Lieutenant-Governor). No realistic second Adams G. Archibald with this honour-and-place profile exists.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous Adams G. Archibald (e.g. a son with the identical forename) holding a K.C.M.G. and Nova Scotia office in the 1880s, or proof that the 1889 M.P. row belongs to a different Archibald whose records were conflated into this stint.

notes: Single bio, single stint. Stint header claims listing 1877-1914 but year-by-year records shown run only 1886-1890; the subject died in the early 1890s, so any later rows would be stale or absent — the visible records are all consistent with this one person. Distinctive full forename 'Adams G' + K.C.M.G. with award-consistent first appearance (1886, awarded 1885) + Nova Scotia place agreement constitute multiple independent dimensions.
